text: Polarized training produces the greatest increase in VO2peak among endurance training approaches, with an 11.7% improvement over 9 weeks compared to threshold, high-volume, or high-intensity interval training alone.
Polarized training increases time to exhaustion by 17.4% and peak velocity/power by 5.1% over 9 weeks, outperforming threshold, high-volume, and HIIT-only approaches.
Both polarized training and HIIT improve lactate threshold (velocity/power at 4 mmol·L⁻¹), but polarized training produces a larger gain (+8.1%) than HIIT (+5.6%).
Threshold-only and high-volume-only training do not produce further improvements in endurance performance variables in athletes who are already well-trained.
HIIT-only training reduces body mass by 3.7% over 9 weeks, while polarized, threshold, and high-volume training do not significantly change body mass.
No training approach (polarized, threshold, HIIT, or high-volume) produces superior improvements in work economy over 9 weeks, suggesting economy is resistant to short-term training concept changes.
